if (!require(shiny)) install.packages("shiny")
if (!require(shinyjs)) install.packages("shinyjs")

library(shiny)
library(shinyjs)

# ── HELPER: loose string similarity ──────────────────────────────────────────
# Returns 0–100% match based on word overlap (keyword matching)
score_answer <- function(user, correct) {
  if (nchar(trimws(user)) == 0) return(0)
  clean <- function(s) {
    s <- tolower(s)
    s <- gsub("[^a-z0-9 ]", " ", s)
    unique(unlist(strsplit(trimws(s), "\\s+")))
  }
  # Remove common stop words so content words drive the score
  stops <- c("a","an","the","is","are","was","were","in","of","to","and",
             "or","but","for","with","that","this","it","its","by","from",
             "as","at","on","be","can","may","not","more","also","both",
             "when","which","than","if","into","has","have","been","does",
             "do","used","using","only","most","each","all","any","some",
             "very","so","their","they","them","these","those","there","then",
             "will","would","should","could","per","e","g","i")
  uw <- setdiff(clean(user),    stops)
  cw <- setdiff(clean(correct), stops)
  if (length(cw) == 0) return(0)
  hits <- sum(uw %in% cw)
  round(100 * hits / length(cw))
}

# ── DATA ──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
flashcards <- list(
  list(term="Randomised Controlled Trial (RCT)",
       definition="Participants randomly allocated to intervention or control; randomisation distributes known and unknown confounders. Gold standard for establishing causality.",
       category="Research Methods"),
  list(term="Cluster Randomisation",
       definition="Groups (e.g. schools, GP practices) rather than individuals are randomised. Used to prevent contamination; cluster is the unit of analysis, requiring larger sample sizes.",
       category="Research Methods"),
  list(term="Crossover Trial",
       definition="Participants receive both intervention and control sequentially, acting as their own control. Requires a washout period; only suitable when effects are rapid and reversible.",
       category="Research Methods"),
  list(term="Intention to Treat (ITT)",
       definition="Participants analysed in their original randomised group regardless of actual treatment received. Preserves randomisation and reflects real-world effectiveness.",
       category="Research Methods"),
  list(term="Per Protocol Analysis",
       definition="Analyses only participants who completed their allocated treatment. Provides the best estimate of efficacy but breaks randomisation, introducing potential confounding.",
       category="Research Methods"),
  list(term="Efficacy vs Effectiveness",
       definition="Efficacy = benefit under ideal (controlled trial) conditions. Effectiveness = real-world benefit where adherence and delivery vary.",
       category="Research Methods"),
  list(term="Cohort Study",
       definition="Exposed and unexposed groups are followed over time to measure incidence. Directly calculates relative risk; susceptible to loss to follow-up bias.",
       category="Research Methods"),
  list(term="Case-Control Study",
       definition="Cases (with disease) and controls (without) are compared for prior exposures. Calculates odds ratio; efficient for rare diseases; susceptible to recall bias.",
       category="Research Methods"),
  list(term="Cross-Sectional Study",
       definition="Exposure and outcome measured simultaneously — a 'snapshot'. Estimates prevalence but cannot establish temporality or causality.",
       category="Research Methods"),
  list(term="Ecological Study",
       definition="Uses group-level (aggregate) data to examine associations. Cheap and uses routine data; key limitation is the ecological fallacy.",
       category="Research Methods"),
  list(term="Ecological Fallacy",
       definition="Wrongly inferring individual-level associations from group-level data. The fundamental limitation of ecological studies.",
       category="Research Methods"),
  list(term="Systematic Review",
       definition="Uses explicit, pre-specified methods to identify and synthesise all evidence on a question. More rigorous than a narrative review; quality depends on included studies.",
       category="Research Methods"),
  list(term="Meta-Analysis",
       definition="Statistical pooling of results from multiple studies to produce a single summary estimate. Only appropriate when studies are sufficiently homogeneous.",
       category="Research Methods"),
  list(term="Grey Literature",
       definition="Non-peer-reviewed material (reports, agency documents, websites) not identified by standard database searches. Must be sought in systematic reviews to reduce publication bias.",
       category="Research Methods"),
  list(term="Heterogeneity (I²)",
       definition="Variability in results across studies beyond chance. I² <20% = minimal; 30–50% = moderate; >70% = severe. High I² makes pooling inappropriate.",
       category="Research Methods"),
  list(term="Funnel Plot",
       definition="Plots effect size against sample size to detect publication bias. Asymmetry suggests smaller negative studies are missing.",
       category="Research Methods"),
  list(term="Publication Bias",
       definition="Positive/significant results are more likely to be published. Inflates pooled effect estimates in meta-analyses; detected by funnel plot asymmetry.",
       category="Research Methods"),
  list(term="Confounding",
       definition="A variable associated with both the exposure and outcome (but not on the causal pathway) that distorts the apparent association. Controlled by randomisation, restriction, matching, or multivariable analysis.",
       category="Research Methods"),
  list(term="Selection Bias",
       definition="Systematic error in how participants are selected or retained, producing a non-representative sample. Examples: self-selection, loss to follow-up, healthy worker effect.",
       category="Research Methods"),
  list(term="Information Bias",
       definition="Systematic error in measuring exposure or outcome. Includes recall bias (cases recall more than controls) and observer bias. Non-differential misclassification biases towards the null.",
       category="Research Methods"),
  list(term="Bradford Hill Criteria",
       definition="Nine criteria to assess causality: strength, consistency, specificity, temporality, dose-response, plausibility, coherence, experiment, analogy. Temporality is the only essential criterion.",
       category="Research Methods"),
  list(term="Relative Risk (Risk Ratio)",
       definition="Risk in exposed ÷ risk in unexposed. RR=1 means no association; RR>1 means increased risk. Directly calculated in cohort studies.",
       category="Research Methods"),
  list(term="Odds Ratio",
       definition="Odds of exposure in cases ÷ odds of exposure in controls. Approximates RR when disease is rare. The measure of association from case-control studies.",
       category="Research Methods"),
  list(term="Attributable Risk (Risk Difference)",
       definition="Incidence in exposed minus incidence in unexposed. Quantifies excess risk due to the exposure; useful for estimating public health impact.",
       category="Research Methods"),
  list(term="Population Attributable Risk",
       definition="Reduction in incidence if the whole population were unexposed. Accounts for both strength of association and exposure prevalence; guides priority-setting.",
       category="Research Methods"),
  list(term="Number Needed to Treat (NNT)",
       definition="1 ÷ absolute risk reduction. Patients needing treatment to prevent one additional adverse outcome. Lower = more effective treatment.",
       category="Research Methods"),
  list(term="Absolute Risk Reduction (ARR)",
       definition="Risk in control group minus risk in intervention group. More informative than relative risk reduction for clinical decision-making.",
       category="Research Methods"),
  list(term="Relative Risk Reduction (RRR)",
       definition="ARR ÷ control group risk. Always appears larger than ARR and can mislead if baseline risk is not stated.",
       category="Research Methods"),
  list(term="Confidence Interval",
       definition="Range within which the true population value is estimated to lie (95% CI excludes null at p<0.05). Conveys both magnitude and precision of an estimate.",
       category="Research Methods"),
  list(term="P-Value",
       definition="Probability of results as extreme as observed if the null hypothesis were true. p<0.05 is statistically significant but does not indicate clinical importance.",
       category="Research Methods"),
  list(term="Type I Error (α)",
       definition="Rejecting a true null hypothesis (false positive). Conventional threshold: α=0.05. Multiple comparisons inflate this risk.",
       category="Research Methods"),
  list(term="Type II Error (β)",
       definition="Failing to reject a false null hypothesis (false negative). Power = 1–β; typically 80–90%.",
       category="Research Methods"),
  list(term="Statistical Power",
       definition="Probability of detecting a true effect if one exists. Increases with sample size, effect size, and measurement precision.",
       category="Research Methods"),
  list(term="Sample Size Calculation",
       definition="For prevalence studies: needs expected prevalence and desired CI width. For RCTs: needs effect size, SD, α, and power.",
       category="Research Methods"),
  list(term="Kaplan-Meier Survival Analysis",
       definition="Non-parametric method estimating time-to-event probability, accounting for censoring. Groups compared using the log-rank test.",
       category="Research Methods"),
  list(term="Cox Proportional Hazards Regression",
       definition="Semi-parametric survival model estimating hazard ratios for multiple covariates simultaneously. Assumes proportional hazards over time.",
       category="Research Methods"),
  list(term="Logistic Regression",
       definition="Models binary outcomes as a function of multiple predictors. Produces adjusted odds ratios; used to control for confounders in observational studies.",
       category="Research Methods"),
  list(term="Poisson Regression",
       definition="Models count data or incidence rates. Produces incidence rate ratios; used in ecological studies and time-series analysis of rare events.",
       category="Research Methods"),
  list(term="Regression to the Mean",
       definition="Extreme values tend to return towards the mean on repeat measurement without intervention. A pitfall in uncontrolled before-and-after studies.",
       category="Research Methods"),
  list(term="Sensitivity Analysis",
       definition="Tests robustness of findings by varying key assumptions or analytical choices. Stable conclusions across assumptions increase confidence in results.",
       category="Research Methods"),
  list(term="Stratification",
       definition="Controlling for confounding by analysing the exposure-outcome association within subgroups defined by the confounder. Reduces power in each stratum.",
       category="Research Methods"),
  list(term="Matching",
       definition="Controls are selected to match cases on potential confounders. Matching must be accounted for in analysis (e.g. conditional logistic regression).",
       category="Research Methods"),
  list(term="Normal Distribution",
       definition="Symmetrical bell-shaped distribution. ~68% values within ±1 SD; ~95% within ±1.96 SDs. Assumed by parametric tests.",
       category="Statistics"),
  list(term="Skewed Distribution",
       definition="Asymmetrical distribution. Positive skew: mean > median (tail right, e.g. length of stay). Use median and IQR for skewed data.",
       category="Statistics"),
  list(term="Standard Deviation",
       definition="Square root of variance; measures spread of individual data around the mean.",
       category="Statistics"),
  list(term="Standard Error",
       definition="SD ÷ √n. Measures precision of the sample mean; used to calculate confidence intervals.",
       category="Statistics"),
  list(term="Interquartile Range (IQR)",
       definition="25th to 75th percentile — the middle 50% of data. Robust measure of spread for skewed or non-normal distributions.",
       category="Statistics"),
  list(term="Chi-Squared Test",
       definition="Tests association between two categorical variables. Requires expected frequencies ≥5; use Fisher's exact test otherwise.",
       category="Statistics"),
  list(term="Independent Samples t-Test",
       definition="Compares means of a continuous variable between two independent groups. Assumes normality and approximately equal variances.",
       category="Statistics"),
  list(term="Paired t-Test",
       definition="Compares means in the same subjects at two time points. Requires differences to be normally distributed.",
       category="Statistics"),
  list(term="Mann-Whitney U Test",
       definition="Non-parametric alternative to the independent t-test. Used when data are ordinal or non-normally distributed.",
       category="Statistics"),
  list(term="Pearson's Correlation (r)",
       definition="Measures strength and direction of a linear relationship (−1 to +1). Does not imply causality; r≈0 does not rule out non-linear associations.",
       category="Statistics"),
  list(term="Spearman's Rank Correlation",
       definition="Non-parametric correlation using ranked data. Used when data are ordinal or not normally distributed.",
       category="Statistics"),
  list(term="Forest Plot",
       definition="Displays individual study results and the pooled estimate in a meta-analysis. Box size ∝ study weight; diamond = pooled effect.",
       category="Statistics"),
  list(term="Outlier",
       definition="Observation far from the main data. Check for measurement/coding error first; if genuine, report sensitivity analysis including and excluding it.",
       category="Statistics"),
  list(term="Sensitivity",
       definition="True positives ÷ (true positives + false negatives). High sensitivity → good at ruling OUT disease (SnNout).",
       category="Diagnostic Tests"),
  list(term="Specificity",
       definition="True negatives ÷ (true negatives + false positives). High specificity → good at ruling IN disease (SpPin).",
       category="Diagnostic Tests"),
  list(term="Positive Predictive Value (PPV)",
       definition="True positives ÷ all positives. Falls with lower disease prevalence even if sensitivity and specificity are unchanged.",
       category="Diagnostic Tests"),
  list(term="Negative Predictive Value (NPV)",
       definition="True negatives ÷ all negatives. Rises as prevalence falls.",
       category="Diagnostic Tests"),
  list(term="Likelihood Ratio Positive (LR+)",
       definition="Sensitivity ÷ (1−specificity). LR+ >10 provides strong evidence for disease. Independent of prevalence.",
       category="Diagnostic Tests"),
  list(term="Likelihood Ratio Negative (LR−)",
       definition="(1−sensitivity) ÷ specificity. LR− <0.1 provides strong evidence against disease. Independent of prevalence.",
       category="Diagnostic Tests"),
  list(term="ROC Curve",
       definition="Plots sensitivity vs. (1−specificity) across thresholds. AUC=1 is perfect; AUC=0.5 is no better than chance.",
       category="Diagnostic Tests"),
  list(term="Wilson & Jungner Criteria",
       definition="Ten criteria for a population screening programme: important condition; recognisable latent stage; natural history understood; accepted treatment; facilities available; suitable test; agreed treatment policy; cost-effective; case-finding continuous; benefits outweigh harms.",
       category="Diagnostic Tests"),
  list(term="Lead Time Bias",
       definition="Screen detection appears to extend survival only because the clock starts earlier, not because death is delayed. Overestimates screening benefit.",
       category="Diagnostic Tests"),
  list(term="Length Time Bias",
       definition="Screening preferentially detects slow-growing, less aggressive tumours. Makes screen-detected cancers appear to have better prognosis.",
       category="Diagnostic Tests"),
  list(term="Overdiagnosis",
       definition="Detection of cancers that would never have caused symptoms or death. Patients receive harmful treatment with no benefit. Major concern in PSA and breast screening.",
       category="Diagnostic Tests"),
  list(term="Incidence",
       definition="New cases arising in a defined population over a specified time. Measures risk of developing disease; used to evaluate preventive interventions.",
       category="Epidemiology"),
  list(term="Prevalence",
       definition="Proportion of a population with a condition at a given time. Prevalence ≈ incidence × duration. Used for planning chronic disease services.",
       category="Epidemiology"),
  list(term="Crude Death Rate",
       definition="Total deaths ÷ mid-year population × 1,000. Not adjusted for age structure; unsuitable for direct comparison between populations.",
       category="Epidemiology"),
  list(term="Direct Standardisation",
       definition="Age-specific rates of the study population applied to a standard population's age structure. Enables fair comparison across populations with different age distributions.",
       category="Epidemiology"),
  list(term="Indirect Standardisation / SMR",
       definition="Standard population's age-specific rates applied to study population's age structure. SMR = (observed ÷ expected) × 100; SMR >100 = excess mortality.",
       category="Epidemiology"),
  list(term="Years of Life Lost (YLL)",
       definition="Measures premature mortality by counting life-years lost relative to standard life expectancy. Weights deaths in younger people more heavily.",
       category="Epidemiology"),
  list(term="DALY",
       definition="Years of Life Lost (YLL) + Years Lived with Disability (YLD), weighted by severity. One DALY = one year of healthy life lost.",
       category="Epidemiology"),
  list(term="QALY",
       definition="Combines quantity and quality of life (0=death, 1=perfect health). NICE threshold ~£20,000–£30,000/QALY in England.",
       category="Epidemiology"),
  list(term="Life Expectancy",
       definition="Average years a person of a given age is expected to live based on current age-specific mortality rates. Requires age-specific mortality data and population estimates.",
       category="Epidemiology"),
  list(term="Healthy Life Expectancy",
       definition="Years expected to live in good health, combining mortality data with age-specific disability/health status. Requires multi-category disability weighting for full marks.",
       category="Epidemiology"),
  list(term="Compression of Morbidity",
       definition="Healthy life expectancy increases as a proportion of total life expectancy — people live longer AND healthier. Opposite = expansion of morbidity.",
       category="Epidemiology"),
  list(term="Person-Time",
       definition="Combines individuals and variable follow-up duration as the denominator for incidence rates (e.g. person-years). Allows valid rate calculation when follow-up times differ.",
       category="Epidemiology"),
  list(term="Attack Rate",
       definition="Cases ÷ exposed individuals × 100. Used in outbreak cohort studies to compare food-specific attack rates and identify the source.",
       category="Epidemiology"),
  list(term="Case Definition",
       definition="Standardised clinical, laboratory, time, place, and person criteria used to classify cases. Balance sensitivity (find all cases) vs. specificity (exclude non-cases).",
       category="Epidemiology"),
  list(term="Epidemic Curve",
       definition="Histogram of case onset times. Narrow peak = point-source outbreak; propagated waves = person-to-person spread; plateau = continuous common source.",
       category="Epidemiology"),
  list(term="Basic Reproduction Number (R₀)",
       definition="Average secondary cases from one case in a fully susceptible population. R₀>1 → epidemic spreads. Determines herd immunity threshold: HIT = 1 − 1/R₀.",
       category="Epidemiology"),
  list(term="Herd Immunity Threshold",
       definition="Proportion of population that must be immune to interrupt transmission. HIT = 1 − 1/R₀. For measles (R₀ ≈ 12–18): ~94% immunity required.",
       category="Epidemiology"),
  list(term="Notifiable Disease",
       definition="Disease legally required to be reported to public health authorities upon diagnosis. Enables surveillance and timely public health response.",
       category="Epidemiology"),
  list(term="Meningococcal Disease",
       definition="Caused by Neisseria meningitidis; presents with fever, neck stiffness, photophobia, non-blanching rash. Manage with immediate benzylpenicillin; ciprofloxacin prophylaxis for close contacts.",
       category="Infectious Diseases"),
  list(term="Measles",
       definition="Highly infectious paramyxovirus; incubation ~10 days; Koplik's spots then maculopapular rash. Herd immunity threshold ~94%; two MMR doses required.",
       category="Infectious Diseases"),
  list(term="HPV",
       definition="Sexually transmitted; high-risk types 16 and 18 cause >70% of UK cervical cancers. UK immunisation programme (Gardasil) offered to all 12–13-year-olds.",
       category="Infectious Diseases"),
  list(term="E. coli O157 (VTEC)",
       definition="Zoonotic infection; reservoir in cattle. Can cause haemolytic uraemic syndrome (HUS). Both HUS and infectious bloody diarrhoea are notifiable in England.",
       category="Infectious Diseases"),
  list(term="Hepatitis C",
       definition="Blood-borne RNA virus; most commonly transmitted via injecting drug use. ~80% develop chronic infection; curative DAA therapy now available. No vaccine.",
       category="Infectious Diseases"),
  list(term="Tuberculosis (TB)",
       definition="Mycobacterium tuberculosis; respiratory droplet transmission. Key risk factors: HIV, deprivation, migration from high-incidence countries. Control: notification, contact tracing, BCG, DOTS.",
       category="Infectious Diseases"),
  list(term="Chlamydia",
       definition="Most common bacterial STI in the UK; mostly asymptomatic. Can cause PID, tubal infertility, and ectopic pregnancy. National Chlamydia Screening Programme in England.",
       category="Infectious Diseases"),
  list(term="Vaccine Efficacy vs Effectiveness",
       definition="Efficacy = protection under ideal (trial) conditions. Effectiveness = real-world protection; may be lower due to cold chain, adherence, and population differences.",
       category="Infectious Diseases"),
  list(term="Cancer Registry",
       definition="Database of all new cancer cases in a defined population. Used for surveillance, service planning, evaluating screening, and research. Raises data protection concerns.",
       category="Health Information"),
  list(term="Disease Register",
       definition="Database of individuals with a long-term condition. Key features: defined population, clear case definition, high registration rate. Used for audit, recall, and service planning.",
       category="Health Information"),
  list(term="Passive Surveillance",
       definition="Clinicians/labs report notifiable cases as they occur. Cheap but under-reports due to incomplete case ascertainment and variable reporting behaviour.",
       category="Health Information"),
  list(term="Active Surveillance",
       definition="Public health authorities proactively contact providers to seek case information. More complete and timely than passive surveillance but more resource-intensive.",
       category="Health Information"),
  list(term="Sentinel Surveillance",
       definition="Selected representative sites (sentinels) monitor trends over time. Efficient but not population-representative. Example: RCGP influenza surveillance network.",
       category="Health Information"),
  list(term="Total Fertility Rate (TFR)",
       definition="Average number of children a woman would have in her lifetime at current age-specific fertility rates. TFR ~2.1 = replacement level in high-income countries.",
       category="Health Information"),
  list(term="Information Governance",
       definition="Framework of law and policy ensuring personal information is managed securely and confidentially. Key elements: GDPR, Data Protection Act, Caldicott Guardian, electronic security.",
       category="Health Information"),
  list(term="Routine Data Sources",
       definition="Administrative data from routine service delivery (e.g. HES, death certificates, cancer registrations, QOF). Advantages: large, continuous, cheap. Disadvantages: limited variables, coding errors.",
       category="Health Information"),
  list(term="Opportunity Cost",
       definition="The value of the next best alternative foregone when a resource allocation decision is made. The fundamental concept underlying all economic evaluation in healthcare.",
       category="Health Economics"),
  list(term="Discounting",
       definition="Future costs and benefits adjusted to present value (UK discount rate 3.5%/year). Critical in screening: costs now, benefits decades later.",
       category="Health Economics"),
  list(term="Cost-Effectiveness Analysis",
       definition="Compares costs against health outcomes (e.g. cost per life-year gained). Does not assign monetary values to health; enables comparison of similar interventions.",
       category="Health Economics"),
  list(term="Cost-Utility Analysis",
       definition="Measures outcomes in QALYs; results as cost per QALY. NICE threshold ~£20,000–£30,000/QALY in England.",
       category="Health Economics"),
  list(term="Cost-Benefit Analysis",
       definition="All costs and outcomes valued in monetary terms. Enables comparison with non-health investments but ethically difficult to monetise human life.",
       category="Health Economics"),
  list(term="Marginal Analysis / PBMA",
       definition="Examines the additional cost and benefit of expanding/reducing a programme by one unit. Used in PBMA to guide resource reallocation decisions.",
       category="Health Economics"),
  list(term="Return on Investment (ROI)",
       definition="(Benefit − Cost) ÷ Cost. Used in public health to demonstrate value of preventive programmes. Social Return on Investment (SROI) includes wider societal benefits.",
       category="Health Economics"),
  list(term="Sick Role (Parsons)",
       definition="Rights: exempt from blame; exempt from normal responsibilities. Obligations: want to recover; seek medical help. Criticised for not accounting for chronic illness.",
       category="Medical Sociology"),
  list(term="Social Iatrogenesis (Illich)",
       definition="Medicalisation undermines individuals' capacity to cope with illness independently. Part of Illich's broader critique of the 'industrialisation' of medicine.",
       category="Medical Sociology"),
  list(term="Social Model of Disability",
       definition="Disability caused by societal barriers (physical, attitudinal, organisational), not the impairment itself. Contrasts with the medical model (problem within individual).",
       category="Medical Sociology"),
  list(term="Medical Model of Disability",
       definition="Disability seen as individual deficit requiring compensation or treatment. Leads to segregated services; criticised for fostering dependence and reducing autonomy.",
       category="Medical Sociology"),
  list(term="Health Locus of Control",
       definition="Internal: health determined by own behaviour. External: determined by chance or powerful others. Internal locus associated with greater uptake of preventive behaviours.",
       category="Medical Sociology"),
  list(term="Inverse Care Law (Tudor Hart)",
       definition="Those in greatest need have least access to good-quality care. Key driver of health inequalities in both primary and secondary care.",
       category="Medical Sociology"),
  list(term="Dahlgren & Whitehead Rainbow Model",
       definition="Concentric layers of health determinants: individual biology → lifestyle → social networks → living/working conditions → macroeconomic and environmental context.",
       category="Medical Sociology"),
  list(term="Health Inequalities",
       definition="Systematic, avoidable, unfair differences in health between social groups. Driven by social determinants. Marmot Review (2010) identified life course action as key.",
       category="Medical Sociology"),
  list(term="Social Determinants of Health",
       definition="Conditions in which people are born, grow, live, work, and age. Include income, education, employment, and housing. Primary drivers of health inequalities.",
       category="Medical Sociology"),
  list(term="Globalisation and Health",
       definition="Political: constrains national health policy (WTO/EU rules). Economic: global health workforce migration, multinational pharma pricing. Social: raised expectations, dietary westernisation.",
       category="Medical Sociology"),
  list(term="Ottawa Charter",
       definition="WHO 1986: five action areas — build healthy public policy; create supportive environments; strengthen community action; develop personal skills; reorient health services.",
       category="Health Promotion"),
  list(term="Stages of Change (Transtheoretical Model)",
       definition="Five stages: precontemplation → contemplation → preparation → action → maintenance. Interventions should be tailored to current stage; relapse is normal.",
       category="Health Promotion"),
  list(term="Health Belief Model",
       definition="Behaviour determined by perceived susceptibility, severity, benefits, barriers, cues to action, and self-efficacy. Widely used in vaccination and screening uptake interventions.",
       category="Health Promotion"),
  list(term="Needs Assessment",
       definition="Three approaches: epidemiological (incidence/prevalence + evidence); comparative (benchmarking); corporate (stakeholder views). Informs commissioning decisions.",
       category="Health Promotion"),
  list(term="Health Impact Assessment (HIA)",
       definition="Prospective appraisal of health impacts of a policy or project. Stages: screening → scoping → appraisal → reporting → monitoring. Considers vulnerable groups.",
       category="Health Promotion"),
  list(term="Social Marketing",
       definition="Applies commercial marketing principles to voluntary behaviour change. Key principles: customer orientation, segmentation, exchange theory, insight, competition.",
       category="Health Promotion"),
  list(term="Maslow's Hierarchy of Needs",
       definition="Five-level hierarchy: physiological → safety → social/belonging → esteem → self-actualisation. Lower-order needs must be broadly met before higher-order needs motivate.",
       category="Management"),
  list(term="Herzberg's Two-Factor Theory",
       definition="Motivators (intrinsic: achievement, recognition, responsibility) drive satisfaction. Hygiene factors (extrinsic: pay, conditions) prevent dissatisfaction but do not motivate.",
       category="Management"),
  list(term="McGregor Theory X and Theory Y",
       definition="Theory X: workers are lazy, require control. Theory Y: workers are self-motivated, thrive with autonomy. Management style shapes culture and performance.",
       category="Management"),
  list(term="Tuckman's Group Development",
       definition="Five stages: forming → storming → norming → performing → adjourning. Leaders should adapt style to the team's developmental stage.",
       category="Management"),
  list(term="SMART Targets",
       definition="Specific, Measurable, Achievable, Relevant, Time-bound. Disadvantages: may incentivise gaming; focus on measurable at expense of important; targets may conflict.",
       category="Management"),
  list(term="Strategy vs Policy",
       definition="Strategy = high-level plan setting direction and goals. Policy = agreed set of measures (e.g. legislation, guidance) contributing to the strategy.",
       category="Management"),
  list(term="Donabedian Model",
       definition="Structure (resources) → Process (clinical activities) → Outcome (health impact). Framework for healthcare quality evaluation. Structure is necessary but not sufficient.",
       category="Management"),
  list(term="Clinical Governance",
       definition="Framework for NHS accountability and continuous quality improvement. Components: clinical audit, evidence-based practice, risk management, patient involvement, training.",
       category="Management"),
  list(term="Logic Model",
       definition="Depicts inputs → activities → outputs → short/medium/long-term outcomes. Makes the theory of change explicit and identifies appropriate evaluation measures.",
       category="Management"),
  list(term="NHS Breast Screening",
       definition="3-yearly mammography for women aged 50–70. ~20% mortality reduction in invited women. Harms: overdiagnosis and false positive biopsies.",
       category="Screening"),
  list(term="NHS Cervical Screening",
       definition="Primary HPV testing with cytology triage. Ages 25–49: 3-yearly; 50–64: 5-yearly. Detects CIN (pre-invasive lesions). HPV vaccination will reduce future screening need.",
       category="Screening"),
  list(term="NHS Bowel Cancer Screening",
       definition="FIT offered to adults aged 50–74; colonoscopy if positive. Detects colorectal cancer early and removes adenomatous polyps.",
       category="Screening"),
  list(term="NHS AAA Screening",
       definition="Single ultrasound scan for men aged 65. Detects asymptomatic aneurysms; rupture carries ~80% mortality. Women excluded (6× lower risk than men).",
       category="Screening"),
  list(term="Marmot Review (2010)",
       definition="Health inequalities are driven by social inequalities and are avoidable. Six policy objectives across the life course, with emphasis on early years and employment.",
       category="Social Policy"),
  list(term="Acheson Report (1998)",
       definition="Independent inquiry documenting widening UK health inequalities. Prioritised policies for families with children, income inequality, and health impact evaluation of all policies.",
       category="Social Policy"),
  list(term="Black Report (1980)",
       definition="Documented social class gradient in UK health. Four explanations: artefact; social selection; cultural/behavioural; materialist/structural (most important).",
       category="Social Policy"),
  list(term="Wanless Report (2002)",
       definition="Modelled three NHS funding scenarios; 'fully engaged' (high public health investment) was most cost-effective. Highlighted importance of evidence-based public health spending.",
       category="Social Policy")
,
  # ── ANSWER FRAMEWORKS (Appendix C) ──────────────────────────────────────────
  list(term="DTOSC – How would you implement…?",
       definition="Define (what it is and its purpose), Team (who is involved and their roles), Operational aspects (day-to-day delivery, resources, timelines), Strategic aspects (long-term goals, governance, integration with wider strategy), Communication (stakeholder engagement, public messaging, reporting). Use this framework for any implementation question.",
       category="Answer Frameworks"),
  list(term="DAPADE – Write short notes on…",
       definition="Define (clear definition of the topic), Application (where/how it is used in public health), PH importance (why it matters for population health), Advantages (strengths of the approach), Disadvantages (limitations and harms), Example (a real-world illustration). Use this for 'write short notes on X' questions.",
       category="Answer Frameworks"),
  list(term="HIPSIAE – Roles of public health",
       definition="Health Improvement (reducing inequalities, promoting wellbeing), Health Protection (communicable disease, emergency preparedness, environmental hazards), Health Services (commissioning, quality, evaluation), Intelligence (surveillance, data, epidemiology), Academic (research, evidence, training), Equity (addressing unfair differences in health). Describes the six domains of public health practice.",
       category="Answer Frameworks"),
  list(term="PPRE – Needs assessment / 'How would you do a needs assessment?'",
       definition="Profiling the situation (epidemiological, comparative and corporate data; burden, trends, comparators), Prioritising options (criteria: effectiveness, cost-effectiveness, feasibility, equity, stakeholder views), Recommendations (evidence-based, SMART, realistic), Evaluation (process and outcome measures, KPIs, re-assessment cycle).",
       category="Answer Frameworks"),
  list(term="PP SS – Identifying needs for a group or health effects of a risk factor",
       definition="Physical health needs: Prevention (primary/secondary/tertiary), Lifestyle factors, Primary care needs, Specialist services; Psychological needs (mental health, wellbeing, behaviour change); Social needs (housing, employment, social support); Special groups (children, elderly, ethnic minorities, homeless, prisoners). Use to structure a comprehensive needs analysis.",
       category="Answer Frameworks"),
  list(term="ECC – Identifying needs for a disease",
       definition="Epidemiological needs (incidence, prevalence, burden, risk factors, outcomes), Comparators (benchmarking against similar populations or national rates), Corporate needs (views of clinicians, patients, commissioners, community). The three classical approaches to health needs assessment (Stevens & Raftery).",
       category="Answer Frameworks"),
  list(term="SPO – Donabedian evaluation of services",
       definition="Structure (the resources needed: staff, equipment, premises, funding, IT), Process (clinical and organisational delivery: guidelines followed, patient journey, waiting times), Outcomes (hard impacts: mortality, morbidity; soft impacts: patient satisfaction, quality of life). Each element is necessary but not sufficient alone.",
       category="Answer Frameworks"),
  list(term="A3E3 – Maxwell's six dimensions of service evaluation",
       definition="Access (can people who need it get it?), Acceptability (is it acceptable to patients and staff?), Appropriateness (is it the right service for the need?), Equity (is it fair across groups?), Efficiency/Economy (best use of resources?), Effectiveness (does it achieve its intended outcomes?). Maxwell's 1984 framework for evaluating health services.",
       category="Answer Frameworks"),
  list(term="PD3RA – Steps of evaluation",
       definition="Purpose (what is being evaluated and why), Dimensions (which aspects: process, outcomes, equity, efficiency), Data (what data to collect and from where), Design (study design: before-after, RCT, cohort, case-control), Reporting (to whom, in what format), Action (how findings will be used to improve services).",
       category="Answer Frameworks"),
  list(term="HAD EPO – Performance monitoring framework",
       definition="Health improved? (population-level outcomes), Access (equitable access across groups), Delivery (process measures, throughput), Efficiency (cost per outcome, activity), Patient experience (satisfaction, complaints, PROMs), Outcomes (hard clinical outcomes). Used for ongoing performance monitoring in both average and inequality terms.",
       category="Answer Frameworks"),
  list(term="SSAREM – Health impact assessment framework",
       definition="Screening (is an HIA warranted? size of task vs benefit), Scoping (define scope, who is affected, steering group, methodology), Appraisal (assess positive/negative impacts; burden; equity; risks; evidence review), Recommendations (to maximise benefits, minimise harms, address inequalities), Engaging decision makers (presenting findings, influencing policy), Monitoring (implementation and outcomes). Standard HIA process.",
       category="Answer Frameworks"),
  list(term="CITPPR – Describing the epidemiology of NCDs",
       definition="Clinical features (presentation, diagnosis, natural history), Importance (mortality, morbidity, burden: DALYs, QALYs, cost), Time trends (secular trends, cohort effects), Place (international, national, regional, deprivation gradient), Person (age, sex, ethnicity, socioeconomic group), Risk factors (modifiable and non-modifiable, PAR). Use for any 'describe the epidemiology of X' question.",
       category="Answer Frameworks"),
  list(term="CURB CC – Reasons for an observed association",
       definition="Causal (genuine causal relationship, meets Bradford Hill criteria), Underlying trend (secular or confounding trend), Reverse causation (outcome causes the exposure), Bias (selection, information, or recall bias), Chance (random error, type I error), Confounding (third variable associated with both). Essential for interpreting any observed association.",
       category="Answer Frameworks"),
  list(term="DPSEEA – Systems approach to environmental health",
       definition="Driver (underlying social, economic, political forces), Pressure (factors that modify the environment: emissions, land use), State (quality of the modified environment: air, water, soil quality), Exposure (human contact with the modified environment), Effects (health outcomes: disease, injury, disability), Actions (interventions at each level to interrupt pathways). WHO framework for environmental health.",
       category="Answer Frameworks"),
  list(term="HDE – Risk assessment",
       definition="Hazard identification (what is the agent and what harm can it cause?), Dose-response assessment (what is the relationship between exposure level and health effect?), Exposure assessment (what are the actual levels of human exposure?). The three core steps before risk characterisation and management.",
       category="Answer Frameworks"),
  list(term="SPR – Pollution/contamination risk assessment",
       definition="Source (origin of the contaminant: industrial site, landfill, agriculture), Pathway (route from source to receptor: air, groundwater, soil, food chain), Receptor (the person or population exposed: residents, workers, vulnerable groups). All three elements must be present for a risk to exist; breaking any link prevents harm.",
       category="Answer Frameworks"),
  list(term="ECCM – Risk management",
       definition="Evaluation of risk (characterise risk: likelihood × severity, acceptable risk threshold), Communication of risk (clear, proportionate messaging to public and stakeholders), Control (SPPPS: Source controls, Pathway controls, Person/Population controls, Primary prevention, Secondary prevention), Monitoring (ongoing surveillance to detect changes and evaluate effectiveness of controls).",
       category="Answer Frameworks"),
  list(term="CCDC DHTCC – Outbreak management",
       definition="Confirm outbreak exists (cluster vs expected baseline), Confirm diagnoses (clinical and microbiological verification), Define case (time, place, person criteria; possible/probable/confirmed), Count (line list, attack rates), Describe data (epidemic curve, TPP), Hypothesis (likely pathogen, source, transmission route), Test hypothesis (case-control/cohort studies, microbiological sampling), Control (isolation, contact tracing, source removal), Communicate (public, media, agencies, UKHSA).",
       category="Answer Frameworks"),
  list(term="ACE DR TSC – Epidemiology and control of infection",
       definition="Agent (organism type, characteristics), Clinical features (incubation, symptoms, severity, complications), Epidemiology (Time/Place/Person distribution), Diagnostic tests (clinical, microbiological), Reservoir (human, animal, environment), Transmission (route: droplet, contact, faeco-oral, vector), Surveillance (notification, laboratory reports), Control: Prevention general/specific (vaccination, hygiene), Case management, Contacts (tracing and prophylaxis), Source (decontamination, removal).",
       category="Answer Frameworks"),
  list(term="APP DRR – Emergency preparedness and response",
       definition="Assess risks (hazard identification, risk matrix, community vulnerability), Prevention (SPPPS: source/pathway/person controls + legislative + educational measures), Preparedness (resources, emergency plan, training, exercises, mutual aid agreements), Detection system (surveillance, early warning, escalation triggers), Response (incident command, evacuation, treatment, communication), Recovery (restoration of services, psychological support, debrief, lessons learned).",
       category="Answer Frameworks"),
  list(term="AS ROME – Developing a health promotion programme",
       definition="Assess need and determinants (epidemiological, comparative, corporate; Dahlgren/Whitehead layers), Stakeholder analysis and engagement (mapping, involvement), Resources (human, financial, infrastructure), Objectives and targets (SMART, short/medium/long-term), Methods (LESS: legislative, empowerment, socio-environmental, specific interventions), Evaluation (process, outcome, economic; logic model). Key framework for programme development questions.",
       category="Answer Frameworks"),
  list(term="SLSLS – Dahlgren & Whitehead determinants of health",
       definition="Socioeconomic, cultural and environmental conditions (outermost layer: macro-economy, environment), Living and working conditions (employment, education, housing, healthcare), Social and community networks (social support, social capital), Lifestyle factors (diet, exercise, smoking, alcohol — influenced by social norms), Sex/age/constitutional factors (innermost fixed layer). The 'rainbow model' — each layer is amenable to intervention.",
       category="Answer Frameworks"),
  list(term="Strengthen ICAS – Dahlgren & Whitehead framework for action",
       definition="Strengthen Individuals (skills, knowledge, health literacy, behaviour change), strengthen Communities (social networks, community development, reducing isolation), improve Access to essential services (healthcare, education, social care), promote Socioeconomic and environmental changes (income, housing, employment, taxation, regulation). Maps onto the Dahlgren/Whitehead rainbow layers.",
       category="Answer Frameworks"),
  list(term="LESS – Methods of health promotion",
       definition="Legislative methods (laws, regulations, fiscal measures: tobacco taxation, seat belt law), Empowerment/informational methods (health education, social marketing, campaigns), Socio-environmental methods (making healthy choices easier: cycle lanes, food environments), Specific interventions (Behavioural therapies, medical interventions, helplines, stop smoking services). Use to structure a comprehensive health promotion response.",
       category="Answer Frameworks"),
  list(term="DM MR NP – Routine health information sources",
       definition="Demographic/social sources (births, deaths, census, migration), Mortality data (death certificates, ONS), Morbidity data (HES, primary care, disease registers, cancer registry, vaccine and screening coverage), Sources covering Risk factors (health surveys, QOF, synthetic estimates), Non-health service sources (council data, emergency services, food supply data), Private sector sources (Experian, supermarket loyalty card data, social media). Used for health needs assessment.",
       category="Answer Frameworks"),
  list(term="BCHR – Uses of collected health data",
       definition="Burden (quantifying the extent of disease and need in a population), Comparisons (benchmarking against other populations, time trends, national standards), Health services (planning, commissioning, monitoring performance, audit), Research and evaluation (evidence generation, programme evaluation, hypothesis testing). Any data source can be assessed against these four purposes.",
       category="Answer Frameworks"),
  list(term="Compare CCCARAT – Limitations of a health data source",
       definition="Compare (how does it compare to other sources/gold standard?), Costs (resource implications of collection and analysis), Consent/confidentiality (data protection issues, opt-out, GDPR), Completeness (coverage, case ascertainment, missing data), Accuracy (validity of coding, clinical coding errors, ICD misclassification), Reliability (consistency over time and between recorders), Accessibility (availability, linkage potential, access restrictions), Timeliness (lag from event to data availability). Essential for critiquing any data source.",
       category="Answer Frameworks"),
  list(term="A PIE – Policy formation and implementation",
       definition="Agenda setting (how the issue gets onto the policy agenda: evidence, media, events, advocacy; Kingdon's streams model), Policy formulation (evidence review; HIA for options; identifying local barriers; stakeholder consultation), Implementation (interpreting policy into local actions; organisational change; application and monitoring), Evaluation and new agenda (assessing impact, identifying gaps, feeding back into the policy cycle). Core policy cycle framework.",
       category="Answer Frameworks"),
  list(term="McKinsey 7S – Describing an organisation",
       definition="Shared values (central: culture and ethos), Strategy (long-term plan to achieve vision), Structure (hierarchy, reporting lines, organisational design), Staff (types, numbers, roles, skill mix), Skills (competencies and capabilities of the workforce), Systems (procedures, planning processes, IT, governance arrangements), Style (leadership and cultural style — see Handy's model: power, task, role, person cultures). Used to analyse or describe any organisation comprehensively.",
       category="Answer Frameworks"),
  list(term="I AM – Stakeholder analysis",
       definition="Identification (list all internal and external stakeholders), Assessment (map on a matrix of influence vs interest — high influence/high interest: manage closely; low influence/low interest: monitor), Manage/Monitor (strategies for engagement: inform, consult, collaborate, empower). Essential for any question about implementation or programme development.",
       category="Answer Frameworks"),
  list(term="POCCC – Fayol's functions of management",
       definition="Plan (set objectives and strategies), Organise (allocate resources and structure), Command (direct and lead staff), Coordinate (align activities across teams), Control (monitor performance against targets and take corrective action). Fayol's classical management theory; contrasts with more modern participative approaches.",
       category="Answer Frameworks"),
  list(term="ADKAR – Change management model",
       definition="Awareness (of the need for change), Desire (to support and participate in the change), Knowledge (how to change), Ability (to implement the change day-to-day), Reinforcement (to sustain the change). Prosci ADKAR model; used for individual-level change management. Complements Lewin's force field and Kotter's 8-step models.",
       category="Answer Frameworks"),
  list(term="4-Step Model – Strategy development",
       definition="Where are we now? (situation analysis: HNA, internal SWOT/McKinsey 7S, external PESTELI/stakeholder analysis), Where do we want to be? (vision, aims, SMART objectives), How will we get there? (options appraisal using evaluation criteria; Lewin force field analysis; route map/action plan), How will we know we're there? (KPIs, milestones, evaluation framework, feedback loop). Core strategic planning cycle.",
       category="Answer Frameworks"),
  list(term="PPPE – Commissioning / Prioritisation cycle",
       definition="Profiling (situation analysis: population needs, current services, gaps), Prioritisation (options appraisal: criteria include effectiveness, cost-effectiveness, equity, feasibility, stakeholder views; tools: programme budgeting, marginal analysis), Procurement (specifying, tendering, contracting, provider selection), Evaluation (monitoring performance, outcomes, value for money, and feeding into next profiling phase).",
       category="Answer Frameworks"),
  list(term="A Planned Briefing Saves Looking CRap – Writing a briefing on paper findings",
       definition="Appropriate language (audience-appropriate, plain English for non-specialists), Purpose (why this briefing is needed), Background (importance of the topic, what is already known, relevant policies), Summary of paper (key findings, strengths, weaknesses, generalisability, what it adds to evidence), Local implementation (stakeholder views, feasibility, pilot considerations, resource requirements, cost-effectiveness), Conclusions and recommendations (clear, actionable, prioritised). Use for 'write a briefing based on these findings' questions.",
       category="Answer Frameworks"),
  list(term="AT SOLO – Writing a letter",
       definition="Appropriate language (tone and vocabulary suited to recipient), Thank for interest and describe local situation (contextualise the query), Summary of evidence (key findings, strengths, weaknesses, practical issues with applying evidence), Other factors and interventions (what else is available or relevant), Local application and implementation (how findings apply locally, barriers, facilitators), Offer further discussion (open invitation to follow up). Use for 'write a letter to a GP/politician/manager' questions.",
       category="Answer Frameworks"),

  # ── HIGH-YIELD ADDITIONAL CONCEPTS (Appendix D topics and textbook) ────────
  list(term="Prevention Paradox (Rose)",
       definition="A preventive measure that brings large benefits to the community offers little to each participating individual. High-risk strategies protect individuals but have little population impact; whole-population strategies (e.g. salt reduction) have large population impact. Explains the tension between individual and population approaches.",
       category="Health Promotion"),
  list(term="Upstream vs Downstream Interventions",
       definition="Downstream: individual-level behavioural interventions (e.g. smoking cessation clinics). Upstream: structural/social determinants (e.g. minimum unit pricing, housing policy). Upstream interventions reach more people and address root causes; downstream are more immediately measurable. Effective public health requires both.",
       category="Health Promotion"),
  list(term="PESTELI – External environment analysis",
       definition="Political (government priorities, legislation), Economic (funding, austerity, market forces), Social (demographics, culture, public expectations), Technological (innovation, digital health, telemedicine), Environmental (climate, sustainability), Legal (regulation, employment law), International (global health threats, trade, migration). Used in the 'where are we now?' phase of strategy development.",
       category="Management"),
  list(term="SWOT Analysis",
       definition="Strengths (internal positives), Weaknesses (internal negatives), Opportunities (external positives), Threats (external negatives). Internal factors are within organisational control; external are outside it. Used in the 'where are we now?' phase alongside PESTELI for a complete situation analysis.",
       category="Management"),
  list(term="Lewin's Force Field Analysis",
       definition="Any situation is maintained by a balance of driving forces (pushing for change) and restraining forces (resisting change). To achieve change: strengthen drivers, weaken restrainers, or both. Three-step model: unfreeze (create readiness) → change (implement) → refreeze (embed new practice).",
       category="Management"),
  list(term="Handy's Model of Organisational Culture",
       definition="Power culture (centralised control, spider-web structure; fast but risky), Task culture (project-based, matrix structure; flexible), Role culture (bureaucratic, hierarchical; stable but slow to change), Person culture (individuals are central; professional partnerships). Understanding culture is key to managing change effectively.",
       category="Management"),
  list(term="Kotter's 8-Step Change Model",
       definition="Create urgency; build a guiding coalition; form vision and strategy; communicate the vision; empower action; generate short-term wins; consolidate gains; anchor changes in culture. Complements ADKAR (individual-level) with an organisational-level change roadmap.",
       category="Management"),
  list(term="Programme Budgeting and Marginal Analysis (PBMA)",
       definition="Programme budgeting: maps current spend against disease/population groups. Marginal analysis: considers what to disinvest (where least benefit at margin) and what to invest (where most benefit at margin). Enables evidence-based resource reallocation within a fixed budget.",
       category="Health Economics"),
  list(term="Incremental Cost-Effectiveness Ratio (ICER)",
       definition="(Cost of new intervention − Cost of comparator) ÷ (Effect of new − Effect of comparator). Expressed as cost per QALY. Compare against NICE threshold (~£20,000–£30,000/QALY). Below threshold = cost-effective; above = not routinely recommended.",
       category="Health Economics"),
  list(term="Sensitivity, Specificity and the 2x2 Table",
       definition="True Positive (TP): disease present, test positive. True Negative (TN): disease absent, test negative. False Positive (FP): disease absent, test positive. False Negative (FN): disease present, test negative. Sensitivity = TP/(TP+FN); Specificity = TN/(TN+FP); PPV = TP/(TP+FP); NPV = TN/(TN+FN). Prevalence affects PPV and NPV but not sensitivity/specificity.",
       category="Diagnostic Tests"),
  list(term="Bayes' Theorem in Screening",
       definition="Post-test probability (PPV) depends on pre-test probability (prevalence), sensitivity, and specificity. Even a highly sensitive and specific test has poor PPV when prevalence is very low. This is why population screening for rare conditions produces many false positives.",
       category="Diagnostic Tests"),
  list(term="Qualitative Methods Overview",
       definition="Used to explore 'why' and 'how' questions. Methods: focus groups (group dynamics, norms), interviews (in-depth individual views), observation (naturalistic behaviour), document analysis. Analysis: thematic analysis, grounded theory, IPA. Strengths: rich contextual data, hypothesis-generating. Weaknesses: not generalisable, subject to interviewer bias, resource-intensive.",
       category="Research Methods"),
  list(term="Triangulation",
       definition="Using multiple methods, data sources, or investigators to study the same phenomenon. Increases validity and credibility of findings. Common in mixed-methods research combining quantitative and qualitative approaches.",
       category="Research Methods"),
  list(term="Effect Modification (Interaction)",
       definition="The effect of an exposure on an outcome differs across strata of a third variable (the effect modifier). Unlike confounding, effect modification is a real biological phenomenon and should be reported, not adjusted away. Detected by stratification; measured by statistical interaction terms.",
       category="Research Methods"),
  list(term="Mendelian Randomisation",
       definition="Uses genetic variants (instrumental variables) as proxies for modifiable exposures to estimate causal effects. Avoids confounding and reverse causation inherent in observational studies. Assumes the genetic variant affects outcome only through the exposure of interest.",
       category="Research Methods"),
  list(term="Deprivation Indices (IMD, Townsend, Carstairs)",
       definition="IMD (Index of Multiple Deprivation): England's official measure; seven domains (income, employment, education, health, crime, housing, environment). Townsend index: unemployment, car ownership, overcrowding, owner-occupation. Carstairs index: used in Scotland. All are area-level (ecological) measures; subject to ecological fallacy.",
       category="Health Information"),
  list(term="Infant Mortality Rate",
       definition="Deaths aged under 1 year per 1,000 live births per year. Key international indicator of population health and social development. UK rate ~3.7/1,000; highest in deprived groups. Associated with poverty, maternal health, congenital anomalies, and sudden infant death.",
       category="Epidemiology"),
  list(term="Perinatal Mortality Rate",
       definition="Stillbirths plus deaths in the first 7 days of life per 1,000 live births. Sensitive indicator of obstetric and neonatal care quality. Key target of MBRRACE-UK confidential inquiry.",
       category="Epidemiology"),
  list(term="Secular Trend",
       definition="Long-term change in disease frequency over decades, independent of short-term variation. Examples: declining cardiovascular mortality since 1970s, rising obesity rates. Distinguishing secular trends from cohort effects and period effects is important for causal inference.",
       category="Epidemiology"),
  list(term="Cohort Effect",
       definition="Variation in disease risk associated with year of birth rather than current age or calendar period. Reflects shared exposures during formative years (e.g. smoking uptake in a birth cohort). Distinguished from period effects (affecting all ages simultaneously).",
       category="Epidemiology"),
  list(term="Immunisation Schedule (UK)",
       definition="Key vaccines: 2/3/4 months (6-in-1/Men B/Rotavirus/PCV), 12–13 months (MMR/Men B booster/PCV booster/Hib-MenC), 3–4 years (4-in-1 preschool booster/MMR), 12–13 years (HPV), 14 years (3-in-1 teen booster/MenACWY). Annual flu for 2–17 year olds. Flu and pneumococcal for ≥65.",
       category="Infectious Diseases"),
  list(term="Contact Tracing and Prophylaxis",
       definition="Close contacts of certain infections require prophylaxis or follow-up. Meningococcal: ciprofloxacin (adults) or rifampicin (children) for household contacts. TB: Mantoux/IGRA testing, INH prophylaxis if LTBI. Hepatitis B: HBIG + vaccination. HIV: post-exposure prophylaxis within 72 hours.",
       category="Infectious Diseases"),
  list(term="Norovirus",
       definition="Most common cause of infectious gastroenteritis outbreaks in UK (healthcare settings, schools, cruise ships). RNA virus; highly infectious (low inoculum dose), resistant to alcohol gel. Control: isolation/cohorting, enhanced cleaning with chlorine-based disinfectants, exclusion of symptomatic staff for 48 hours after symptom resolution.",
       category="Infectious Diseases"),
  list(term="Salmonella",
       definition="Gram-negative rod; zoonotic reservoir in poultry, eggs, reptiles. Causes diarrhoea, fever, vomiting. Non-typhoidal Salmonella is notifiable; typhoidal (S. typhi) causes typhoid fever. Control: food hygiene, cooking temperatures, contact tracing for food handlers. Antibiotics only for severe/invasive disease.",
       category="Infectious Diseases"),
  list(term="Campylobacter",
       definition="Most common bacterial cause of foodborne illness in the UK. Reservoir: poultry (especially chicken liver). Incubation 2–5 days; self-limiting bloody diarrhoea. Notifiable. Control: food hygiene, cooking poultry thoroughly. Rarely requires antibiotics (azithromycin if needed).",
       category="Infectious Diseases"),
  list(term="Legionella",
       definition="Legionella pneumophila; environmental organism in warm water systems (cooling towers, hot water tanks). Legionnaires' disease: severe pneumonia, notifiable. Pontiac fever: milder flu-like illness. Control: water temperature maintenance (>60°C storage, >50°C delivery), regular flushing, biocide treatment, risk assessment under HSE ACOP L8.",
       category="Infectious Diseases"),
  list(term="Listeria",
       definition="Listeria monocytogenes; foodborne pathogen in chilled ready-to-eat foods (pâté, soft cheeses, smoked salmon). High mortality in pregnancy (foetal loss), neonates, elderly, immunocompromised. Grows at refrigeration temperatures. Notifiable. Control: food safety regulations, advice to high-risk groups.",
       category="Infectious Diseases"),
  list(term="MRSA",
       definition="Meticillin-resistant Staphylococcus aureus. Healthcare-associated infection. Risk factors: invasive devices, surgery, prolonged hospital stay. Control: hand hygiene (alcohol gel/soap), decolonisation (chlorhexidine wash + nasal mupirocin), contact precautions, mandatory surveillance and reporting in NHS. Treated with vancomycin or teicoplanin.",
       category="Infectious Diseases"),
  list(term="C. difficile (CDI)",
       definition="Clostridium difficile infection: antibiotic-associated colitis, especially with broad-spectrum antibiotics. Spore-forming; alcohol gel-resistant — hand hygiene with soap and water essential. Control: antibiotic stewardship, isolation, environmental cleaning with sporicidal agents (chlorine-based). Treated with metronidazole (mild) or vancomycin/fidaxomicin (severe).",
       category="Infectious Diseases"),
  list(term="Influenza Pandemic Preparedness",
       definition="WHO phases: interpandemic → alert → pandemic → transition. UK response: NHS surge capacity, antivirals stockpile (oseltamivir), accelerated vaccine development, communications. Key lessons from 2009 H1N1: early severity assessment, targeted vaccination, healthcare worker protection. Requires international surveillance (WHO GISRS).",
       category="Infectious Diseases"),
  list(term="Herd Immunity and Vaccine Uptake",
       definition="To achieve herd immunity: measles requires ~95% coverage (R₀ ~12–18), polio ~85%, seasonal flu lower due to antigenic drift. Vaccine hesitancy (WHO top 10 global health threats) is driven by complacency, inconvenience, and lack of confidence. Strategies: trusted messengers, reducing access barriers, addressing misinformation.",
       category="Infectious Diseases"),
  list(term="Climate Change and Health",
       definition="Direct effects: heat-related illness, flooding injuries, cold-related deaths. Indirect effects: expanded vector range (Lyme disease, dengue), food insecurity, air pollution, mental health impacts of extreme weather. Health adaptation: heatwave plans, flood resilience, sustainable food systems. Health professionals have a role in mitigation advocacy.",
       category="Environmental Health"),
  list(term="Air Quality and Health",
       definition="Key pollutants: PM2.5 (cardiovascular and respiratory disease — no safe threshold), NO2 (respiratory — mainly from transport), O3 (photochemical smog — exacerbates asthma). WHO 2021 guidelines tightened limits. UK monitoring: Defra AURN network. Clean Air Zones (CAZs) introduced in multiple cities. Disproportionate burden on deprived communities.",
       category="Environmental Health"),
  list(term="Water Safety and Fluoridation",
       definition="Safe water requires treatment (flocculation, sedimentation, filtration, chlorination). Drinking Water Inspectorate (DWI) regulates in England/Wales. Water fluoridation at 1mg/L reduces dental caries; public health benefit is well-evidenced. Concerns about dental fluorosis at high levels. Health and Care Act 2022 gives Secretary of State power to mandate fluoridation schemes.",
       category="Environmental Health"),
  list(term="Joint Strategic Needs Assessment (JSNA)",
       definition="Statutory duty on upper-tier local authorities and NHS commissioners (CCGs/ICBs) to jointly assess the current and future health and wellbeing needs of their population. Informs the Joint Health and Wellbeing Strategy. Uses epidemiological, comparative, and corporate needs assessment approaches.",
       category="Health Information"),
  list(term="Health and Wellbeing Boards",
       definition="Statutory bodies in each upper-tier local authority in England (Health and Social Care Act 2012). Bring together local NHS, public health, social care, and elected representatives. Produce the Joint Strategic Needs Assessment and Joint Health and Wellbeing Strategy. Promote integrated commissioning.",
       category="Social Policy"),
  list(term="Integrated Care Systems (ICS)",
       definition="NHS-led partnerships of healthcare providers and commissioners covering defined geographic populations (England). Statutory footing from Health and Care Act 2022. ICBs (Integrated Care Boards) replace CCGs. Aim: improve population health outcomes, reduce inequalities, improve productivity, support social and economic development. Public health directors sit within local government but work closely with ICS.",
       category="Social Policy"),
  list(term="Clinical Audit Cycle",
       definition="Set standards (evidence-based criteria) → Measure current practice against standards → Identify gaps → Implement change → Re-audit (close the loop). Continuous quality improvement tool; part of clinical governance. Differs from research: audit measures against standards; research generates new knowledge.",
       category="Management"),
  list(term="Options Appraisal",
       definition="Systematic comparison of possible interventions against agreed criteria: effectiveness (evidence base), cost-effectiveness (ICER/cost per QALY), feasibility (political, organisational, financial), acceptability (to patients/public/staff), equity (impact on health inequalities), sustainability. Often presented as a weighted scoring matrix.",
       category="Management"),
  list(term="Bevan's Inverse Care Law and Equity",
       definition="Tudor Hart (1971): availability of good medical care varies inversely with the need of the population served. Drives health inequalities. Addressed through: resource allocation formulae (Carr-Hill), deprivation payments in primary care, targeted public health programmes, social prescribing.",
       category="Medical Sociology"),
  list(term="Social Capital",
       definition="Resources available through social networks: bonding capital (within tight-knit groups), bridging capital (between groups), linking capital (across power hierarchies). Higher social capital associated with better health outcomes. Putnam's theory. Public health interventions can build social capital through community development.",
       category="Medical Sociology"),
  list(term="Salutogenesis (Antonovsky)",
       definition="Focus on factors that promote health and wellbeing rather than just preventing disease. Central concept: 'sense of coherence' — comprehensibility (world is understandable), manageability (resources available to cope), meaningfulness (life has meaning). Contrasts with pathogenic (disease-focused) approaches. Underpins asset-based community development.",
       category="Medical Sociology"),
  list(term="Lalonde Report (1974)",
       definition="Canadian government report identifying four health field determinants: human biology, environment, lifestyle, and healthcare organisation. Estimated healthcare organisation contributes least to health; lifestyle and environment most. Foundational for the new public health movement and social determinants approach.",
       category="Social Policy"),
  list(term="Fairness and Freedom (Nuffield Council Ladder of Intervention)",
       definition="Seven rungs from least to most interventionist: do nothing/monitor; provide information; enable choice; guide choice through incentives/disincentives; guide through defaults; restrict choice; eliminate choice. Used to evaluate proportionality of public health interventions relative to individual liberty. Relevant for policy questions.",
       category="Health Promotion")

)

categories <- sort(unique(sapply(flashcards, function(x) x$category)))
N <- length(flashcards)
